karaage-kun-yoru.name_jp: からあげクン 夜の味
karaage-kun-yoru.description_jp: 地域《ちいき》限定《げんてい》。どの夜《よる》かは書《か》かれていない。五個《ごこ》。
karaage-kun-yoru.description_en: Regional limited edition. Which night, it does not say. Five pieces.

onigiri-benizake.description_jp: 九月《くがつ》にとれた鮭《さけ》。少し寒《さむ》い土地《とち》の米《こめ》。どちらにも何《なに》も聞《き》かなかった。
onigiri-benizake.description_en: A salmon caught in September. Rice from a slightly colder place. Neither was asked anything.

back.body:
[chokusha]・[kouon-tashitsu]をさけて[hozon]してください
[seizousho]: [miyagi]の[koujou]
[shoumikigen]: [sokumen]に[kisai]

tea-koi-ryokucha.description_jp: もっと濃《こ》くしてほしいと言《い》われた。お茶《ちゃ》は同意《どうい》した。瓶《びん》には525ミリリットルの同意《どうい》が入《はい》っている。
tea-koi-ryokucha.description_en: Asked to be stronger. The tea agreed. The bottle holds 525ml of agreement.

back.body:
[kaifuugo]は[ohayameni][onomi]ください
[chokusha]・[kouon]をさけて[hozon]してください
[seizousha]: [shizuoka]の[koujou]
[youki]は[recycle]できます

blocks.body: [fax]サービスのご案内《あんない》
受付《うけつけ》時間《じかん》: [eigyouchuu]

fax-service.prose:
当店《とうてん》では[fax]の[soushin]・[jushin]を承《うけたまわ》っております。

[kokunai][soushin]: 1枚《まい》 [gojuuen]
[kaigai][soushin]: 1枚《まい》 [nihyakuen]
[jushin]: 1枚《まい》 [sanjuuen]

[kaigai]の[soushin]は[kuni]により[toriatsukai]できない場合《ばあい》があります。
詳《くわ》しくは[tenin]までお尋《たず》ねください。

lost-parakeet.posted_by: 近所《きんじょ》の方《かた》
lost-parakeet.prose:
[maigo]になりました。

名前《なまえ》: ケイスケ
種類《しゅるい》: [inko]
色《いろ》: [kiiro]と[midori]
特徴《とくちょう》: [shitsumon]でしか話《はな》しません

[mikaketa]方《かた》は[renraku]ください。

script.body: [otsugi]でお待《ま》ちのお客様《きゃくさま》、どうぞ。
{{change_amount}}円《えん》の[okaeshi]です。[receipt]のご利用《りよう》は?
The doors slide open. You are outside the konbini. Briefly.

ageabura.etymology: 揚げる (to deep-fry) + 油 (oil). Native compound.

aji.etymology: Native Japanese word. Also reads ミ in compounds like 意味 (imi, meaning) and 興味 (kyoumi, interest).
aji.korean_parallel: 맛 (mat) is the native Korean word. 미 (味) appears in Sino-Korean compounds: 의미 (uimi, meaning), 흥미 (heungmi, interest). Parallel layered structure — native word for everyday, Sino reading for compounds.

allergy.etymology: From German Allergie. Arrived in Japanese via medical vocabulary in the early 20th century.
allergy.korean_parallel: 알레르기 (allereugi) — also from German, via similar medical borrowing route.

arigatou.etymology: From 有り難い (arigatai, "difficult to exist" → rare and precious). Originally Buddhist: a thing so rare it deserves gratitude. The modern thank-you preserves the structure but not the awareness of it.

atoaji.korean_parallel: 뒷맛 (dwitmat) — native compound, 뒤 (after) + 맛 (taste). Same structure in both languages using native morphemes.

benizake.etymology: 紅 (crimson) + 鮭 (salmon). The 紅 reading here is べに (native kun'yomi), not the Sino-Japanese コウ seen in 紅茶 (kōcha, black tea).
benizake.korean_parallel: 연어 (yeoneo) is a native Korean word with no hanja parallel to 鮭. 紅 as hong appears in 홍차 (hongcha, black tea) — same 紅茶 compound.

chiiki-gentei.etymology: 地域 (region) + 限定 (limitation). Marketing term ubiquitous on konbini packaging to signal regional exclusivity.
chiiki-gentei.korean_parallel: 지역한정 (jiyeok-hanjeong) — exact cognate, same compound, same marketing usage.

chokusha.etymology: 直射 (direct emission) + 日光 (sunlight). Standard phrase in storage warnings.
chokusha.korean_parallel: 직사광선 (jiksagwangseon) — same construction with 광선 (light rays) instead of 일광. Similar regulatory register.

choumiryo.etymology: 調 (adjust) + 味 (flavor) + 料 (material). On ingredient lists, often followed by parenthetical specifying the type (e.g. アミノ酸等).

chui.etymology: 注 (pour, focus) + 意 (intention, mind). "Pour one's attention."

daijoubu.meaning_en: okay, no thank you (in konbini context, often means "no need")
daijoubu.etymology: 大 (great) + 丈夫 (sturdy/healthy). Originally meant "fine, in good order." In modern Japanese it has also become a polite way to decline ("I'm fine without it") — a source of endless confusion for learners.
daijoubu.korean_parallel: 대장부 (daejangbu) exists in Korean with the original meaning (a strong man). The Japanese semantic drift to "okay/no thanks" did not occur in Korean.

daizu.etymology: 大 (large) + 豆 (bean). Listed as an allergen on Japanese packaging.
daizu.korean_parallel: 대두 (daedu) — direct cognate, used in formal/agricultural contexts. Everyday Korean uses native 콩 (kong).

denpun.etymology: 澱 (sediment) + 粉 (powder). 澱 is non-jōyō so typically written でん on modern packaging.
denpun.korean_parallel: 전분 (jeonbun) — direct cognate using the same compound.

eigyouchuu.etymology: 営業 (business operation) + 中 (middle, during). Standard sign on open shops.
eigyouchuu.korean_parallel: 영업중 (yeongeopjung) — direct cognate, identical signage usage.

energy.etymology: From German Energie. Japanese scientific vocabulary often took German over English in the Meiji-Shōwa period. Always first line on nutrition panels.
energy.korean_parallel: 에너지 (eneoji) — from English. A rare case where Japanese took German and Korean took English for the same scientific term.

fax.etymology: English loanword. Konbini fax services persisted in Japan long after they disappeared elsewhere.
fax.korean_parallel: 팩스 (paekseu) — also from English, shorter form.

fukumu.etymology: Native Japanese verb. On allergen labels always appears in the form 〜を含む (contains X).
fukumu.korean_parallel: 포함하다 (pohamhada) from 包含 — different verb, similar function. 含 appears in 함유 (hamyu, containing) on Korean ingredient labels.

genzairyo.etymology: 原 (origin) + 材料 (material) + 名 (name). Mandated by Japanese food labeling law.
genzairyo.korean_parallel: 원재료명 (wonjaeryomyeong) — direct cognate. Korean food labels use the same exact compound.

gojuuen.etymology: 五十 (fifty) + 円 (yen). 円 was originally the general term for a round object, borrowed as the currency unit in 1871.

gokoiri.etymology: 五 (five) + 個 (piece-counter) + 入り (contained). 入り attaches to numbers to mean "containing N."

goukei.etymology: 合 (combine) + 計 (count). Standard cashier/register vocabulary.

hozon.etymology: 保 (keep, protect) + 存 (exist). "Keep existing." Standard in both packaging warnings and digital context (file save).
hozon.korean_parallel: 보존 (bojon) — direct cognate. Korean uses 저장 (jeojang) for digital file save, 보존 for physical preservation — same split.

hyouji.etymology: 表 (surface, show) + 示 (indicate). Regulatory term; 表示 paired with other nouns forms standardized phrases (栄養成分表示, アレルギー表示).
hyouji.korean_parallel: 표시 (pyosi) — direct cognate with identical compound usage.

inko.etymology: Written in katakana despite being an older loanword (possibly from Dutch or early Portuguese via earlier trade). Small parrots.
inko.korean_parallel: 잉꼬 (inkko) — borrowed from Japanese, not directly from a European source.

irasshaimase.etymology: Honorific imperative of いらっしゃる (to come/go, honorific). The polite imperative -ませ ending is rare outside customer service contexts.
irasshaimase.korean_parallel: 어서오세요 (eoseo oseyo) — functionally equivalent. Korean uses an honorific imperative of 오다 (to come).

isshoni.etymology: 一 (one) + 緒 (thread/beginning) + に (particle). "In one thread." Often collocates with 食事 for "together with a meal."

jikabiyaki.etymology: 直 (direct) + 火 (fire) + 焼 (grill). 焼 takes the native kun'yomi やき. A packaging/restaurant term signaling flame contact rather than oven or pan cooking.
jikabiyaki.korean_parallel: 직화구이 (jikhwa-gui) — exact cognate. 직 (直) + 화 (火) + native 구이 (grilling). Same compound structure up to the native-word tail.

jushin.etymology: 受 (receive) + 信 (signal, message). Paired with 送信 (soushin, sending).
jushin.korean_parallel: 수신 (susin) — direct cognate, same usage in postal and telecom contexts.

kaifuugo.etymology: 開封 (opening a seal) + 後 (after). 封 originally meant "seal a letter." Standard storage warning phrase.

kaigai.etymology: 海 (sea) + 外 (outside). "Beyond the sea." Japan's historical island perspective preserved in the everyday word for "international."
kaigai.korean_parallel: 해외 (haeoe) — direct cognate with identical logic and usage.

karaage-kun.etymology: 唐揚げ (karaage, Chinese-style deep-fried) + クン (kun, a familiar suffix for boys/younger males). A 1986 Lawson product whose name stuck. The クン is why the product feels like a small friend rather than food.

katechin.etymology: From English/Latin "catechin," itself from Malay kachu (the plant Acacia catechu). Appears frequently on Japanese tea marketing.
katechin.korean_parallel: 카테킨 (katekin) — also from the same international scientific term.

kiiro.etymology: 黄 (yellow) + 色 (color). Color names in Japanese often take 色 as a suffix for the noun form.
kiiro.korean_parallel: 노랑/노란색 (norang/noransaek) uses a native Korean root. 황 (黃) appears in Sino-Korean compounds like 황금 (hwanggeum, gold) but not in everyday color names.

kisai.etymology: 記 (record) + 載 (place upon, include). "Placed on record." Formal/regulatory register.

koi.meaning_en: strong, concentrated, rich (of flavor or color)
koi.etymology: Native Japanese adjective. Antonym: 薄い (usui, weak/thin).
koi.korean_parallel: 진하다 (jinhada) — native Korean, functionally equivalent. 농 (濃) appears in Sino-Korean compounds like 농도 (nongdo, concentration) but not as a standalone adjective.

kokunai.etymology: 国 (country) + 内 (inside). Antonym of 海外.

kokusan.etymology: 国 (country) + 産 (produced). Extremely productive marketing prefix — 国産牛, 国産野菜, 国産大豆.
kokusan.korean_parallel: 국산 (guksan) — direct cognate, used the same way in Korean marketing.

kokusanchaba.etymology: 国産 (domestic) + 茶葉 (tea leaves). 茶葉 combines 茶 (tea) + 葉 (leaf).

kokusanmai.etymology: 国産 (domestic) + 米 (rice, on'yomi マイ). Sino-Japanese compound; the everyday word for rice-as-grain is こめ.
kokusanmai.korean_parallel: 국산미 (guksan-mi) exists but less common than 국산쌀 (guksan-ssal) using native 쌀 for rice. Same pattern, different habitual usage.

kome.etymology: Native kun'yomi こめ for rice-as-grain. The on'yomi マイ/ベイ appears in compounds: 米国 (America), 新米 (new rice), 精米 (polished rice). Cooked rice is a different word: ご飯 (gohan).
kome.korean_parallel: 쌀 (ssal) is the native Korean word for uncooked rice — no hanja. 미 (米) as a hanja reading appears in compounds: 미국 (America). Both languages preserve a native word for the staple.

komugi.etymology: 小 (small) + 麦 (barley/grain). "Small barley" — older botanical taxonomy.
komugi.korean_parallel: 밀 (mil) is native Korean. 소맥 (somaek) from 小麦 exists but is technical/formal.

komugiko.korean_parallel: 밀가루 (milgaru) — native compound, 밀 (wheat) + 가루 (powder). Same structure with native morphemes.

koromo.meaning_en: batter, coating (on fried food); also "clothing" in classical usage
koromo.etymology: Originally "clothing," now used for the outer coating of food. The image: the food is wearing a garment of batter.

koujou.etymology: 工 (work, craft) + 場 (place). "Place of work/production."

kouon.etymology: 高 (high) + 温 (warm/temperature). Standard in storage warnings.

kouon-tashitsu.etymology: 高温 (high temperature) + 多湿 (much humidity). Four-kanji compound (四字熟語 structure) used specifically for storage warnings.

koushinryou.etymology: 香 (fragrance) + 辛 (spicy) + 料 (material). "Fragrant spicy material."

kuni.etymology: Native kun'yomi for 国. On'yomi コク appears in compounds (国産, 国内, 国民).
kuni.korean_parallel: 나라 (nara) is native Korean. 국 (國/国) appears in Sino-Korean compounds — the same layered pattern as Japanese.

maigo.meaning_en: lost child, lost (of a person or pet)
maigo.etymology: 迷 (lost, confused) + 子 (child). Originally for children, extended to pets in modern usage.
maigo.korean_parallel: 미아 (mia) from 迷兒 uses the same structure. Both languages use the "lost child" compound for the concept.

midori.etymology: Native kun'yomi. Japanese historically classified blue and green together as 青 (ao); 緑 emerged as a distinct color term relatively recently.
midori.korean_parallel: 녹색 (noksaek) from 緑色 is Sino-Korean. Native 초록 (chorok) and 푸른 (pureun, blue/green) show the same historical color-category mixing.

mikaketa.meaning_en: saw, spotted (past tense of 見かける)
mikaketa.etymology: 見る (see) + かける (auxiliary meaning "happen to" or "do briefly"). "Happen to see." Used in lost-pet notices and witness-seeking contexts.

miyagi.etymology: 宮 (shrine/palace) + 城 (castle) + 県 (prefecture). Northeast Honshu. Sendai is its capital.

muchakushoku.etymology: 無 (without) + 着色 (coloring). The 無 prefix is extremely productive in food marketing: 無糖 (sugar-free), 無添加 (no additives), 無香料 (no fragrance).
muchakushoku.korean_parallel: 무착색 (muchaksaek) — direct cognate. The 무- prefix is equally productive in Korean food marketing.

mukouryou.etymology: 無 (without) + 香料 (fragrance/flavoring). Paired frequently with 無着色 in marketing.

nado.etymology: On ingredient lists, appears after a category to indicate unspecified members. Written with hiragana なんど in older texts; now typically 等 or など.
nado.korean_parallel: 등 (deung) from 等 — same kanji, same function, same ingredient-list usage.

nibai.etymology: 二 (two) + 倍 (times, multiplier). 倍 as a suffix attaches to numbers to form multipliers.
nibai.korean_parallel: 배 (bae) from 倍 — same kanji, same structure (2배, dubbae).

oazukari.etymology: Honorific お- + 預かる (to hold on behalf of). Used specifically in customer-service payment contexts: "I am receiving your X yen" rather than "you gave me X yen."

ohayameni.etymology: Honorific お- + 早め (earlier) + に (adverb particle). Softer than 早く, suitable for packaging instructions.

okaeshi.etymology: Honorific お- + 返し (returning). In transactions means change given back; in social contexts means a return gift.

omeshiagari.etymology: Honorific form of 食べる/飲む. 召す is a classical honorific verb meaning "to do (various things)" — eat, wear, call for, ride. On packaging always お召し上がりください.
omeshiagari.korean_parallel: 드시다 (deusida) is the equivalent Korean honorific for eating/drinking.

onegai.etymology: Honorific お- + 願い (wish, request). Used as a polite way to agree ("yes please") in service exchanges.
onegai.korean_parallel: 부탁 (butak) is the native/Sino-Korean equivalent; different morphology but parallel social function.

onomi.etymology: Honorific お- + 飲み (drink, stem form). Used in instructions (お飲みください, please drink).

osusume.meaning_en: recommendation (also written 御勧め, 御薦め)
osusume.etymology: Honorific お- + 勧め (recommendation, from 勧める to recommend). Common in restaurant and retail contexts; on packaging often in the negative: "we do not recommend X."
osusume.korean_parallel: 추천 (chucheon) from 推薦 is the Sino-Korean equivalent.

otsugi.etymology: Honorific お- + 次 (next). Specific to service register when addressing the next person in line.

receipt.etymology: From English "receipt." Distinct from 領収書 (ryoushuusho), which is a formal receipt with buyer's name for reimbursement or tax purposes. The konbini clerk asks about the former.
receipt.korean_parallel: 영수증 (yeongsujeung) from 領收證 — Korean uses the formal term for both contexts, no common loanword equivalent to レシート.

recycle.etymology: From English "recycle." Always in katakana; 再生 (saisei, rebirth/regeneration) is the native alternative but specialized.
recycle.korean_parallel: 재활용 (jaehwaryong) from 再活用 — Korean uses a Sino-Korean compound ("reuse") rather than an English loanword.

ryokucha.etymology: 緑 (green) + 茶 (tea). The Japanese convention separates 緑茶 (green), 紅茶 (black), and 麦茶 (barley tea) by color/material.
ryokucha.korean_parallel: 녹차 (nokcha) — direct cognate. Same three-way distinction in Korean: 녹차, 홍차, 보리차.

saikanetsu.etymology: 再 (again) + 加熱 (heating, applying heat). 加熱 combines 加 (add) + 熱 (heat).

sake.etymology: Native Japanese word. The kanji 鮭 has 魚 (fish) radical with 圭 (jade tablet) as phonetic component. Colloquial variant しゃけ is common in informal speech and older generations.
sake.korean_parallel: 연어 (yeoneo) — native Korean word, no hanja bridge. A case where CJKV parallel doesn't apply.

seizousha.etymology: 製造 (manufacture) + 者 (person/entity). For corporate entities; for facilities the term is 製造所.

seizousho.etymology: 製造 (manufacture) + 所 (place). Required on food labels when manufactured by a contracted facility.

sharei.etymology: 謝 (apologize, thank) + 礼 (ceremony, courtesy). A formal thank-you often taking monetary form.
sharei.korean_parallel: 사례 (sarye) — direct cognate, same usage in lost-and-found contexts.

shishitsu.etymology: 脂 (fat, grease) + 質 (substance). Paired with the other three macronutrient terms on every nutrition panel.

shitsumon.etymology: 質 (substance, essence) + 問 (ask). "Ask about the substance of."

shizuoka.etymology: 静 (quiet) + 岡 (hill) + 県 (prefecture). Central Honshu, south of Mount Fuji. Major tea-producing region.

shokuen.etymology: 食 (food, eating) + 塩 (salt). Distinguished from 塩 alone (salt in general) to specify salt intended for consumption.
shokuen.korean_parallel: 식염 (sikyeom) — direct cognate, used in regulatory/medical contexts.

shokuen-soto.reading: しょくえんそうとうりょう
shokuen-soto.etymology: 食塩 (table salt) + 相当 (equivalent) + 量 (amount). Japan reports sodium as its salt equivalent rather than raw sodium. Multiply sodium by 2.54 to get this figure.
shokuen-soto.korean_parallel: 식염상당량 (sikyeom-sangdangnyang) — exact compound cognate. Korean food labels use the same regulatory format.

shokuji.etymology: 食 (eat) + 事 (matter, event). "The matter of eating."
shokuji.korean_parallel: 식사 (siksa) — direct cognate using 食事 with different second character reading.

shoumikigen.etymology: 賞味 (appreciate the taste of) + 期限 (time limit). Distinct from 消費期限 (shouhi kigen, safety-critical use-by date). 賞味 implies taste quality, not safety.
shoumikigen.korean_parallel: 유통기한 (yutonggihan, distribution period) is what Korean labels typically show — conceptually similar but different compound.

sokumen.etymology: 側 (side) + 面 (face, surface). Standard packaging phrase: 側面に記載 (noted on the side).

soushin.etymology: 送 (send) + 信 (signal, message). Paired with 受信 (jushin, receiving).

tanpakushitsu.etymology: 蛋白 (egg white) + 質 (substance). Written たんぱく in hiragana on most modern packaging because 蛋 is non-jōyō. Egg whites were the prototype protein in 19th-century biochemistry.
tanpakushitsu.korean_parallel: 단백질 (danbaekjil) — identical compound (蛋白質). Same etymology, same reasoning. One of the cleanest CJK cognates you'll find.

tansuikabutsu.etymology: 炭 (carbon) + 水 (water) + 化 (transform) + 物 (substance). Direct translation of the scientific term "carbohydrate" — a late 19th-century Japanese coinage that then traveled back to Chinese and Korean.
tansuikabutsu.korean_parallel: 탄수화물 (tansuhwamul) — full cognate. Meiji-era Japanese scientific vocabulary is why modern Korean and Chinese scientific terminology often tracks Japanese closely.

toriatsukai.meaning_en: handling, treatment (of an item or service)
toriatsukai.etymology: 取る (take) + 扱う (handle). Compound verb noun. In service contexts: what the business offers or declines to offer.
toriatsukai.korean_parallel: 취급 (chwigeup) from 取扱 — direct cognate with same business usage.

toriniku.etymology: 鶏 (chicken, the bird) + 肉 (meat). Transparent compound.
toriniku.korean_parallel: 닭고기 (dakgogi) — native Korean compound using 닭 (chicken) + 고기 (meat). Same structure with different morphemes.

vitamin-c.etymology: From English "vitamin." On ingredient lists often added as an antioxidant/preservative rather than as a nutritional claim.

yakinori.etymology: 焼く (to toast/grill) + のり (seaweed sheets). のり typically written in hiragana on packaging though 海苔 exists.
yakinori.korean_parallel: 김 (gim) is native Korean. Toasted/seasoned nori is 구운 김. The shared seaweed culture with different names is a good example of parallel-but-independent food traditions.

yoru.etymology: Native kun'yomi. On'yomi ヤ appears in compounds: 夜間 (yakan, nighttime), 深夜 (shin'ya, deep night).
yoru.korean_parallel: 밤 (bam) is native Korean. 야 (夜) appears in Sino-Korean compounds: 심야 (simya, deep night), 야간 (yagan, nighttime) — identical pattern.

youki.etymology: 容 (hold, contain) + 器 (vessel, tool). "Holding vessel."

saved_label.jp: 保存《ほぞん》した言葉《ことば》

first_time_greeting.jp: あなたはコンビニの中《なか》にいます。

empty_basket.jp: レジがさびしがっています。何《なに》か買《か》ってください。
empty_basket.en: The register is lonely. Buy something.

empty_saved_words.jp: まだ何《なに》も保存《ほぞん》していません。それはそれでよろしい。
empty_saved_words.en: You have saved nothing. This is defensible.

empty_board.jp: 掲示板《けいじばん》には何《なに》もありません。
empty_board.en: The bulletin board has nothing on it.

exit_line.jp: 自動《じどう》ドアが開《ひら》きます。あなたはコンビニの外《そと》にいます。しばらくの間《あいだ》だけ。
exit_line.en: The doors slide open. You are outside the konbini. Briefly.

tap_item_hint.jp: 商品《しょうひん》をタップ

export_saved_label.jp: 保存《ほぞん》した言葉《ことば》を書《か》き出《だ》す

about_label.jp: このコンビニについて

clear_data_confirm.jp: 本当《ほんとう》に消《け》しますか?

about_body.jp:
このコンビニは営業《えいぎょう》中《ちゅう》です。いつも営業《えいぎょう》しています。米《こめ》は少《すこ》し寒《さむ》い土地《とち》から来《き》ています。

棚《たな》の上《うえ》のものは、ここにあるという意味《いみ》で存在《そんざい》しています。ほかの場所《ばしょ》に存在《そんざい》するかどうかは、コンビニの関心事《かんしんじ》ではありません。

保存《ほぞん》した言葉《ことば》があれば、書《か》き出《だ》せます。なければ、出《で》ていって構《かま》いません。自動《じどう》ドアは両方向《りょうほうこう》に開《ひら》きます。

— 店長《てんちょう》
about_body.en:
This konbini is open. It has always been open. The rice is from somewhere colder.

Everything on the shelves is real in the sense that it exists here. Whether it exists anywhere else is not the konbini's concern.

If you have saved words, you may export them. If you have not, you may leave. The doors slide both ways.

— Management
